was just also reading Andy's notes, speaking with you as well.

Like, would love to hear about, if you have an example, I mean, I was trying to look at what you and Annie were talking about, but, like, tell me about, like, if you have some dashboards you've built, and, like, how you think about that, and, like, just in general, how you think about, like, using data to, like, push any projects forward you're doing, and, like, I don't know.
I know that's broad, but I'd just love to dive into, like, experience there you've built.

And through the broad lens, I'm going to kind of meander a bit, so pop in and ask questions or tell me to skip parts if I'm getting a bit pedantic or whatever.
But, like, I've never joined an organization that's had perfectly clean data or well-structured data even.
So that's part of, like, what I like to do.

Untangle these messy situations and bring clarity and actionable insights based on like this raw volume of amazing stuff that a company already has in place, like available, not necessarily like established or, you know, structured.
So what I will use as an example is my time at Apollo, which I think I dove deep in with Andy, but it's a great example.
So I'm going to do it again.
When I joined, there was no customer success org whatsoever.
There was no budget for a tool and the data was just crap.
There was way too much of it, way too much because Apollo, what they do is like, find all of this data on the internet and then sell it to other people.
So like we had, right?
Like sales signals?
Um, yeah.
Like clay or common rooms or?
I'm sorry.

Leads.

Yeah.
So LinkedIn, I believe, is one of their data sources.

So company size and the amount of people that are, for instance, in an engineering team at an organization, if it's a B2B product that's selling an engineering tool, we would repackage that data and sell it to that engineering company and be like, hey, this is the total market that you're missing within your existing customer base, that sort of thing.
So we had tons of attributes at the account level, at the contact level, more insights than we knew what to do with.
And I identified a gap almost immediately that our own utilization data was janky, just not good.
We had it piped into Salesforce.
We had some old kind of like archaic definitions as to what it used to mean.
Nobody was keeping up with the flows or macro, whatever Salesforce functionality was built for it at the time to keep it up to date.
And I had to run a customer success team with it.
So I went...
Back to the source, the product management team, and asked for fresh flows.
We didn't have a BI person at the time, I don't think.
We hadn't hired the head of BI, so we had a lower-level analyst that was just making do, but didn't have the organizational know-how to whip the org into shape.
So I had to navigate a bunch of missing components outside of my skill area to even begin getting in the data I needed to create the dashboard.
That meant things to the end users and the executive level.
Ultimately, was able to connect the product to the Snowflake, get the Snowflake, and connect that to Vitally, is what I ultimately implemented at Apollo.
were looking at Yep, yep.

it's like product data.

was like, it would take Salesforce data, product data, whatever.

But what we can do as operators is build a sustainable system and document it so other people can pick it up if we get hit by a bus tomorrow.
Like, that's my theory.

So, got all that connected.
And then I was able to, like, apply meaningful go-to-market data because I had Snowflake data coming into Vitally.
I had our, like, contract data, which had this weird relationship kind of between Stripe and Salesforce.
But, like, I pulled all of that from Salesforce into Vitally.
And then I could like marry them and they were in love.
And we had a like a very light like headcount wise onboarding team, three individuals when I started.
So I say I started the CS team.
I did because it was a true startup and they had three people that were pulled from support and made onboarding that had no structure, no rigor, and just kind of got on the phone with people.

So I, you know, like architected their, their journey, their customer journeys in tandem with them, but then had to like step through iterations as they had to adopt to like more formal structure, but then was able to start guiding them like, okay, within your first two calls, you should see this adoption point at your customers.
Like they should have 50% of their users should have their email inboxes linked up here is where that is present in vitally.
So like giving them the boots on the ground kind of insight as to like how their accounts are performing.
So when they're 10 minutes before they're hopping.
On the phone, we should be able to look at this and see, right?

And then also, like, zooming out then to the executive view, I could say to that manager of CS or my boss, the SVP of RevOps, like, across all of those customers that are onboarding, those that are net new within the last seven days, this is how effective our onboarding team is at scale, like, and draw extrapolations like that.

So, I mean, that was, like, the very first couple of weeks of the Vitality implementation and getting that team all set up, but then ultimately, like, bringing in, you know, renewal data, I implemented a health score, not a, oh, two health scores to start, because I think that onboarding period has a different measure of health than anything beyond that onboarding period.
We need to understand those early risk factors versus, like, they're adopted and now, like, what is their likelihood of renewal sort of thing?
And yeah.
And what's your involvement in, like, the definition?
Like, who, or who drives?
I was like the definition of those things.
It depends on the resources available.
I've totally done it on my own with a hope, prayer, and some logic and hypothesis.
But if I have a resource that is able to help me capacity-wise in BI and run some statistical analyses and stuff, I definitely use that partner to the best of my ability.
We have a very different business model today, but we're going to launch actually two different platforms in the next two to three months.
So it will become more of a product company.
But the way we started, which was very smart of Marcel, our CEO, was like, he's like, I don't want to burn millions of dollars to find a product and hope there's product market fit.
I'm just going to land a bunch of customers today and do everything behind the scenes.
So it's like a services business today.
It's just almost like an agency.
And we learned from them.
So then our team uses our tool today.
Like, it's not exposed to the customers.
But it's an entire platform proprietary we've built where we can see – you would find it very interesting to go look at.
You can see who on our team is using it, how they're using it, if they're using it well.
Like, anyway, we're making it a lot – we improve it as we go because we learn what the customer is like and then build the platform.
So it's like you don't have to, like, burn money to get product market fit.
You kind of, like, have paying customers.
Like, we have, like, 13 million ARR already.
And then we take the money and we can, like, build the platform at the same time.
The very approach – I've normally been in SaaS where it's the opposite, right?
It's, like, just try to, at all costs, get people into the platform and then complain for better or for worse and make it better and better.
But we hide it today.
But then we will be launching it.
So there's, like, almost, like, two different business models.
So…

Which is why it's just more complicated, I think, than most where it's like, we still need the fundamentals of like, account health in the agency and like, customer success metrics and like, our people, you know, but that definition of what success looks like is very different because there's no product signal, there is no product for the customer to use.
A though, like when, I'm just going to speak as though I'm on the team, okay?

Now, write the article, fact check the article, add links in the article.

And it like spits out an article at end, which is then edited by a human.
So it's our editors that go in there today.
I would say there's like the biggest gap in like ability is the people who use it best are very good with prompting and AI.
they understand like the limitations of LLMs or like the importance of the context that it's using in the first place.

Did you give it good documentation?

It does.
It does.

Associate the level of interaction and utilization within the product itself to the output and or satisfaction, how the commercial metrics are on, you know, renewal and all that too.
We could marry those things.

Like, it's all there.

Everything is going to be in one platform at the end.

And then they take the one they want to do and they put it in our content OS, which is our platform.
And then when they want the customer to edit it as well, they dump it into a Google Doc and share it with a customer.
All the edits are in Google Doc.
So the AI is not learning from that, obviously.
If we get our customers into the platform and we're all editing.
Everything within our platform together, then it's learning from that.
And it's like, it will just get so much better at spitting out first drafts that are like 98% ready to go.
So it's like, so the answer is yes, like it's going to be there.
I think parts of it are there.
We can definitely get some efficiency metrics and like deliverability, you know, are we like doing our delivery correctly?
Some of it's going to be like coming from Airtable for a little bit longer and other things.
But anyways, I digress.
That makes sense.

Exactly.

Like, let's just  try one and see what happens.

Anyways, what, tell me, okay, this is philosophical e-question.
I haven't actually thought how I wanted to phrase it, but, like, how do you think about when to automate?
When and versus when to not automate something.
Oh, I've been asked that question so much in my career.
And I used to have a canned answer for it, but I'm a out of practice.
I've been on a personal sabbatical for like the last year and a half.
So I've been out of need to be staffed.
about that next too.
Okay.
When to automate something.
Like, when it becomes, okay, so if I'm automating something for myself as the operator, like if I'm doing it for the second or third time, exact same way that needs to be automated, period.
What I've found with my customer facing teams is they don't get annoyed until like the 80th or 90th time they do something the same.
So I try to proactively when I'm meeting with my stakeholders, suss out, like, what are you repeatedly doing?
Because if you're doing it the exact same way, the exact same time, like by the third time, you should know this needs to be automated.
That's my answer.
Yeah, that's a good answer.
It's actually so true.
One thing we've been spending.
A of time on is just because even our team is not in a platform, it's like just trying to figure out what everyone's doing all day.

Not that I think they're, I know they're all very busy and like keeping customers happy, but like there's no, I can't compare any of the CSMs even or like account managers, whatever the editors, it's like everyone's, there's no apples to apples going on.
So then I don't know, we don't know how to make like, you know, is it true to, can we actually compare this pods output to this groups or whatever?
So yeah, people, it's actually wild to me how long it takes people to realize something is like inefficient.
Yeah.
Even in this era of like AI being the shortcut, like people still haven't, don't think of it as the shortcut.
But, um, the unknown is scary and causes pause and people feel like sometimes it's easier to just start doing something manually.
Yeah.
AI automated systems processes, that sort of.
Like, I have found strength in this career path I've chosen because I'll pause, but then, like, aim to solve at scale to standardize a process for teams of people.
Yeah.

I think it's fun.
Yeah.
Yeah.

you don't optimize for the worst person because then you're just making something silly that shouldn't even be there.
So you kind of have like, adjust the elevation to, like, yeah.

like every day is like, oh, impact literally every day because it's like very easy to identify.

I know you've had the year off, but have you been dabbling in things?
I guess in your past, it would have been a bit more early on, but did you use...

Yeah, at the very front end of this AI boom, I managed a tool called Intercom at Apollo.
I own the support team as well as the CS ops team.
So Intercom was one of the first ones out with an AI chatbot.
I turned that thing on.
It sucked real bad.
I turned it off.
I trained it better.
And then limited its reach because it's like, we didn't issue...
We were a month-to-month sort of like pretty cheap product if you were just at ground floor.
And people would be like, can I get a refund?
And the AI chatbot would be like, yes, here's an internal email address you should not know.
And it just created havoc the first time I turned it on.
So I learned a lot from that first hard fail.
And I trained it up better and limited its scope.
So we didn't have our internal billing team screaming at me.
Um, and yeah, so that just dipped the toe in really, um, since I've been out, um, I haven't done a ton with it.
Like there's some automated tools in so many websites.
on the art side, there's like Fine Art America, which I considered putting his artwork on, but the margins are crap.
So I didn't, but in the meantime, I was testing it out.
Like it applied so many keywords to like a single picture I would upload.
That's neat.

I use it for my resume updates.
Otherwise I would, it would take so long to do everything, but.
Like the Corbett's mine.

So I have like 17 different LLMs I can tap into at any given time.
And I've gotten good at deciphering like the value of each individual and all that.
Yeah.
All right.
Great.
I just, I always want to make sure everyone's excited about it.
And like, we try to be to the extent possible.

It's very hard when you're just running ahead of a moving train, you know, always like every day, but try to like look for AI native tooling, you know, like Marcel's big on like, don't just default to like your old, the tools you've all used for the last 10 years.
Like there's way, probably way better new versions of everything, you know?

Of like using it as your first instinct on things.

you have any questions for me?

We can help drive the definitions.

I didn't train them on it.

Like this is actually not a good system.
And then it makes them not bought into it because they're like, this is so, you have to like make it good enough and like get feedback.
It's almost like, I feel like ops people are like kind of PMs for our own company, like for our own operations.
Like the customer success team is like our customer.
It's like, does it work for you?
Right.
And is it helping make a difference or is it just busy work making you fill out something or whatever?
So I think like, it's that, it's like, we've got, I feel, and I have like the ideas and then it's just like, we don't have the time to go build something and build something that works.
And then try to make it better and like make it work for the rest of the team too.
So there's.
Like there's literally no shortage of things to be worked on.
Like we're so early.

And then it was like, okay, if someone has health flag on their account, what do we do about it?
So then we started, we use linear for tickets.
We're like, okay, let's just like start collecting that.

There's drastic price difference.
There's drastic like ICP difference.

I do.
